forecasts.reasoning: The question asks whether any of the listed candidates (Gnangbo Kacou, Laurent Gbagbo, Pascal Affi N'Guessan, Jean-Louis Billon, or Simone Gbagbo) will be disqualified, removed from the electoral list, or otherwise become legally ineligible to run in the 2025 Ivorian presidential election before July 1, 2025. Search results explicitly confirm that former President Laurent Gbagbo was excluded from the provisional electoral list published by the Independent Electoral Commission (CEI) in March 2025. His exclusion stems from a 2018 Ivorian court conviction sentencing him to 20 years in prison concerning the 'BCEAO robbery,' which strips him of his civic rights. The Ivorian government has refused calls (including from the African Court) to reinstate him. Because Laurent Gbagbo is specifically named in the resolution criteria and is currently both legally ineligible to run and removed from the electoral list as of the cutoff date, the criteria for a YES resolution are securely met. (Additionally, another major opposition figure, Tidjane Thiam, was also disqualified in April 2025, but his exclusion is immaterial to the resolution since Gbagbo's disqualification alone satisfies the 'any of these candidates' condition).

The exclusion of Tesla products from the BC Hydro EV charger rebate program was implemented in mid-March 2025 as a direct retaliatory measure in the ongoing US-Canada trade war, specifically targeting Elon Musk's association with the US administration. As of late May 2025 (approaching the knowledge cutoff), news reports confirm that the trade war between the US and Canada is ongoing and expected to last for the "foreseeable future." Since July 2025 is only a month after the knowledge cutoff, there is very little time for the high-level geopolitical tensions to resolve, for a new policy to be drafted, and for BC Hydro to update its website to re-include Tesla. Additionally, political retaliations tied to "emergency powers" rarely unwind quickly. A reversal by July 1st is highly unlikely.

As of the knowledge cutoff (May 30, 2025), two out of ten New Orleans jail escapees (Antoine Massey and Derrick Groves) remain at large, 14 days after the May 16 jailbreak. The timeline gives authorities exactly one month (until July 1) to capture or kill both men for a YES resolution. Factors favoring their capture include a massive multi-agency manhunt, the arrest of at least 13 accomplices (which severs their logistics and support networks), and a recently increased $50,000 reward each, which strongly incentivizes tips. However, Massey's prior history of escaping and evading capture, the fact they have eluded police for two weeks, and the possibility they have crossed state lines (as two other escapees were caught in Texas) present non-trivial tail risks that they could disappear for longer than 45 total days. Assuming an 80-85% chance for each to be caught in the remaining timeframe, the joint probability falls around 65-70%. I estimate a 68% chance that both are caught or killed before July 1.

The resolution criteria require a signed economic agreement between Lebanon and the IMF by July 1, 2025, where the IMF agrees to provide money. A late April 2025 statement (April 28, 2025) by Lebanon's Economy Minister states the goal of signing only a 'staff-level agreement' (SLA) 'within months'. By definition, an SLA does not commit the IMF to provide money until prior actions are met and the IMF Executive Board approves it. Lebanon previously reached an SLA in April 2022 but spent three years failing to implement its prior actions. With only one month left until the end of the timeline (May 30 to July 1), an SLA itself is stated to be 'months' away, and an actual disbursing loan agreement would take even longer. Additionally, there are no news reports about any emergency rapid financing facility being approved for Lebanon right now. Thus, the probability is extremely close to zero.

The base rate of a U.S. president visiting Russia is historically very low, with no such visit occurring since Obama in 2013. Given the current geopolitical climate and the ongoing war in Ukraine, a presidential visit to Moscow would be extremely politically and logistically controversial and complex. While Trump and Putin have expressed interest in meeting to negotiate an end to the war, diplomatic precedent and recent statements (including the Kremlin mentioning Saudi Arabia as a potential location) strongly suggest that any summit would take place in a neutral third country (e.g., Saudi Arabia, Turkey, or a European nation). Furthermore, news reports updated through May 29, 2025, indicate that Trump has been "unable to coax Russia into peace talks," implying no concrete meeting plan has been solidified. Trump has also relied on his envoy, Steve Witkoff, to travel to Moscow rather than going himself. With only one month left in the resolution window (June 2025) and no scheduled trip on the horizon, the chance of an abrupt, unannounced visit to Russia is exceptionally low.

The administration has clearly demonstrated the intent and capability to arrest/indict James Comey. Earlier in 2025, they installed an interim U.S. Attorney (Lindsey Halligan) who successfully secured a grand jury indictment against Comey for false statements and obstruction. Although a federal judge recently dismissed this indictment 'without prejudice' due to Halligan's invalid appointment, the DOJ retains the ability to refile the charges immediately with a legally appointed prosecutor. Furthermore, Comey is entangled in a fresh controversy as of mid-May 2025 regarding a seashell Instagram post ('86 47'), which triggered a Secret Service probe and explicit calls for his arrest from top officials like DNI Tulsi Gabbard. Given that the DOJ is highly responsive to the President's priorities (evident from Alina Habba recently filing charges against a Democratic representative as acting U.S. Attorney in NJ), the likelihood of the DOJ refiling charges against Comey and booking him before the July 1, 2025 deadline is substantial. There is also a non-trivial chance he was already formally booked (amounting to an arrest) during the processing of the original Halligan indictment. The extremely short ~1-month timeframe remaining (from May 30 to July 1) is the main factor preventing a higher probability, as bureaucratic or legal delays could push a re-indictment past July.
